questions.question: What pricing multiple can done-for-you (DFY) services typically command over self-serve tools?

options.text: The same price with different packaging

questions.explanation: DFY services command 3-10x premiums. A $200/month SaaS becomes a $2,000/month managed service. Customers pay the premium because you're selling back their time, eliminating their learning curve, and guaranteeing execution.

questions.question: What is the magic phrase from a customer that signals a DFY upsell opportunity?

options.text: I just don't have time to do this myself
I need a discount on my subscription

questions.explanation: 'I don't have time to do this myself' is the DFY signal. The customer values the outcome but lacks time or confidence to execute. They're telling you they'll pay 3-10x more to have you do it for them.

questions.question: Which DFY archetype describes a coaching client who completed a program but needs help executing the framework?
questions.explanation: Coaching to DFY Execution is when a student understands the framework but lacks time or confidence to implement it. The offer becomes: 'You've learned the system. For clients who want me to execute it for them, here's my done-for-you package.'

questions.question: Done-for-you services are only viable for service businesses, not SaaS companies.
questions.explanation: False. SaaS companies can offer DFY implementation, configuration, data migration, and ongoing management on top of their software subscription. This is the SaaS-to-DFY Implementation archetype, commanding 3-5x the subscription price.

questions.question: Design a done-for-you upsell offer for your business. Describe: what the customer currently does themselves, what you would do for them in the DFY package, the pricing multiple compared to self-serve, and how you would position the offer when a customer signals interest.
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response identifies a specific DFY offer (not generic), defines clear deliverables that the customer currently struggles with, sets a realistic pricing multiple (3-10x), and positions the offer as solving a time/execution problem rather than as a hard upsell.
